name: The Flyover
subject: Trump Sues BBC, Pennies Worth Millions, and Viral Recipes
body:
President Trump filed a [$10 billion lawsuit](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkD2OwyAQhU-DOyzA2E4Kim3cZdstLX6GmBUGC8axcvsVucJ2nzSap_e9n8f60CGG9PzWOyj0eU1w1QiIUFYs537UE6ox9oCUAtQrF9z2VTAxUi4on1enQ3yvsnPKAwgYOlB8nsb7dB8H0W3KT07ywdwmK72XjskbwCy5BOfsTTPWBdXSuOAz52wceG-kG0dgMADjWnpOJAMXMJf-N4eEG_j4zi8ovc17F9WGeFQyfBGxELFc19VbU5tFuxOxNCRi-cjQqK96BqTGWNrMKGfUhBhDTtSB17vGhmfyOhSKRTugR9EWgwVaoB45VSBi6Ypyx9Ue6zQTyZ67DvFTCP-14kuJvwAAAP__QQGKIg) against the BBC Monday, claiming defamation after the British broadcaster aired doctored footage of Trump in a documentary days before the 2024 election.

| | |
---
| [](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykzzFuwyAUgOHTmC0W7wF2PTBUqtIpHbJ0RNg8x7TYICB2cvuqvUIP8P_S93kxF-uD324fdiVd52g2OkqgWimbmu9rKncq4zgl2jZP5Yi5LqtBjuoEeILeOOvD00jm9EyEJBhp6Ds1dIMSyBYNXPEJRmVd1ylLyipEHF8ETANKFAPz-vcGCD0AVwLaUTqliJMgDlbO0EhOzteY26_ot7rQHJ5xp9xOcWVBL7Wm0ojXBs8Nnu_piPm7TY-59bHB8_X98bZfWdYuHT4EX7q-kfy2Wh_--vov9K7xJwAA__8Rpmrc) | |
# Wednesday, December 17, 2025
---|---
**Good Morning!** On this day in 1903, [Orville and Wilbur Wright](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0D1urDAUxfHVmM7In_AoXLyGbtKmRB58PdzI2Mi-M4jdRxNlB6l_0jnS__O23DwmzI8Pv4OjWJYMZ0tABHWh-tyP9oR2v68H5IzQzlJp2xcllOVScTkuwWO6FtMFFwEU6A6cHAc7DZPVqtvcqPUUhZnAD4NW4781ejUFHa0Hv1phOnTvNankKKWwWvZ3E6wFARqE9CZKZgQEpFL7r4KZNojpKi-o_Vr2LrmN6GhM_2dqZmo-z7PfsFGp19uZmmnDxoO_OGb-K0zNEWsj7rEeyWfgMSG0rrpwnJgStmFkRjx2j-nnhf6U5uXUdwAAAP__BsV6OA) achieved the first successful flight of a self-propelled, heavier-than-air aircraft, flying for 12 seconds and covering 120 feet. Before digital cameras and inflatable décor, snow days meant buckets, scarves, and whatever you could find for a face. [Vintage snowman](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkLFuwyAURb8Gb0SAwTgDQxdv6drRAr9HTIsBAYmVv6_SX-h2pSudI52v23qzIYZ0_7QHmu7zmvBsEXvHuvb6OEp7YHNuK5hSwHbm2vdjFUwoygXlegUb4muVAxiPKHAc0HA9qet0VaMYdgMWmNJoNTgm7DxOTm8zzNpbgGlzegjmTeOCa86ZGvnFSVAKGY7IuJWeE8kQQs_18p1D6jv6-MpPrJctH0M0e--lkfGDiIWIxUfbdmd_3icRS0v5PDBRm4C-Ny2YS0QaEvX5kYCWPffcqJyZnBgRy1ANlDPEGNqkiWT3w4b4Z-r_yvM04jcAAP__P2p6-g) photos in today’s edition capture that era. What kind of eyes did your snowmen have—coal, buttons, or cookies? **Later today:** watch for a partner message from [Elf Labs](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkD2P3CAQQH8N7taC4csuKBKdtru0KRE24zURYAT4rPv3kS9SpNRp5z3NjN7Pd_vuQgz59cMlNH07bMarRewdq-31TKWd2JZlLZhzwHYdte_JAgX5YPBg2noX4qcVgzcbIiAf0DCt5KxmyWHYzezopkHPG0PnQILyXC8TLBoVKuRqCObexoBpxqjkbFyElxIpcqTMiY0RQdGHftTx1xFy33GLn8cH1nE90hDN3ntphH8j8CTwvK5rxLhFt7Sb30P-PHuy7TjrioS_YXIhElAuFcK_3yihD2ci_K244B_F1Z6xtj2Uf6zVpeLCK395fxU20Yeg2v6JcebQneXzpIQCIWEaqvHlCjGGpjQR9HUf_3q8_1ftDwO_AwAA__9InZPf) on why Disney’s $1 billion AI deal matters, and what it could mean for you as an investor. If you’re feeling the year-end crunch, today’s sponsor, [Upwork Business Plus](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykzzFuwyAUgOHTmC0W7wF2PTBUqtIpHbJ0RNg8x7TYICB2cvuqvUIP8P_S93kxF-uD324fdiVd52g2OkqgWimbmu9rKncq4zgl2jZP5Yi5LqtBjuoEeILeOOvD00jm9EyEJBhp6Ds1dIMSyBYNXPEJRmVd1ylLyipEHF8ETANKFAPz-vcGCD0AVwLaUTqliJMgDlbO0EhOzteY26_ot7rQHJ5xp9xOcWVBL7Wm0ojXBs8Nnu_piPm7TY-59bHB8_X98bZfWdYuHT4EX7q-kfy2Wh_--vov9K7xJwAA__8Rpmrc), helps you keep projects moving with fast access to top talent.
|
---
## Fentanyl Named a Weapon of Mass Destruction
President Trump signed an executive order designating illicit [fentanyl as a weapon of mass destruction](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkLGypCAQRb8GM6agFZkJCDYhe5tuaCG0T7YQLGjH9e-3nF94SdcJurrvuX--pi8XU8zfv92GhpYyZTxbQiKsE9Vj29uBbZ79jjlHbGeptG4TCFBcApd6Ci6maxq6YBZEwL5DI_WoXuNL9dCtBmc5Cr-E2c8zeik9Aobw6gWKp1J-7KK5r0mQWkqhevmYh6AUCuxRSDcskg0CQ6RSH39LzLTikq7yxvrwZeuSWYn2xvpfDCwDe57n42iOSnDXvcDANir1YmBvMwZ2LylS9DfejxlYCfe46WPM8R_6g-IbeakBK18wk8tX4ie6vWS-udZ4wEb18BRLZmCfWj9B6FEIzcB21YT9jCnFNmo2iO_NxfSJSz_q-G3gfwAAAP__ngyRXg), directing the Pentagon and Justice Department to take additional steps to combat the drug that killed more than 48,000 Americans in 2024. The order describes fentanyl as “[closer to a chemical weapon](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkDGOwyAURE-DO1vwARMXFNu4y7ZbWth8YlYYLCBBuf3KucJ2I400o_d-7svd-ODj49scqKtLS8RWAtaKean5eZzliWVdtxNj9FhaynU_FqAgewY9U4s1PrwX0VntEAF5h5qpUU7jJDl0u56mm3BMWH4z2wojBaE2Th21YJVi0nVeX2sMmGKMSs6GVVgpkSJHyoxwjAiK1teUh9_kY93RhXd6YR62dHRB77WehfAvAjOBubU2bGu5KK6ewHxFAvMHpncYq4nv0Dc0Z4p9cv1hSuktlpqfW_UpEpi7rO3ZfAi-jIoI-jiMD5-7-i9HLw1_AQAA___9gX0J)” than a narcotic, noting that two milligrams—equivalent to 10 to 15 grains of table salt—can be a lethal dose and could be weaponized for large-scale terror attacks. The designation allows the Pentagon to assist the Justice Department with defense assets to enforce criminal activity related to fentanyl, while the Justice Department pursues harsher penalties, including tougher sentences for trafficking. See [the full executive order here](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0DGypCAQxvHTYIYFjc6sAcEmZm_TDS2URnsLwYJ2qLn91psrvAv8vq_-f7-WL0eR0v7HnWg55CVhqxGZsSxc7vOqN9Z13S5MibC2XPg4F1AwSg1SPxfvKL6XofM2IAKaDq1-PsbpMY0GusNu2nljpl9r8DhOZtsggJ6cR6U0qmHqyH5rGvRTazUa3a-DH0dUaFBpNwQtBoWeOJf-X6bEB4b4zi8s_ZbPLtqD-arC_BYwC5hba307iPHId8V-zy8B81WwksfE5KJ0G1NOVcD8vStg1iBg9lhpT44p7TJgYpfeUboqnWzorpxkDvJ0tUqPlcv9MQTMXbH-ahQj1cdTDGo_HcXPMf5RzZeF_wEAAP__9R-Lyw).
## Trump Sues BBC for $10 Billion
President Trump filed a [$10 billion lawsuit](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkD2OwyAQhU-DOyzA2E4Kim3cZdstLX6GmBUGC8axcvsVucJ2nzSap_e9n8f60CGG9PzWOyj0eU1w1QiIUFYs537UE6ox9oCUAtQrF9z2VTAxUi4on1enQ3yvsnPKAwgYOlB8nsb7dB8H0W3KT07ywdwmK72XjskbwCy5BOfsTTPWBdXSuOAz52wceG-kG0dgMADjWnpOJAMXMJf-N4eEG_j4zi8ovc17F9WGeFQyfBGxELFc19VbU5tFuxOxNCRi-cjQqK96BqTGWNrMKGfUhBhDTtSB17vGhmfyOhSKRTugR9EWgwVaoB45VSBi6Ypyx9Ue6zQTyZ67DvFTCP-14kuJvwAAAP__QQGKIg) against the BBC Monday, claiming defamation after the British broadcaster aired doctored footage of Trump in a documentary days before the 2024 election. The lawsuit alleges the BBC spliced together different portions of his Jan. 6, 2021, speech from nearly an hour apart to suggest Trump incited violence while [omitting clips where](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkb2u3CAQhZ8Gd7PygO1dFy42itzd29wipcXPcE2EwQK8aN8-IlKeIN0czeiTvjm_PrYP6bwL35_yoKXYuAWq2VMplLaSruPMF2Wl9EkhOMo1prIfG-_5CMgB75uRzr-3oTOLJeIkOlrwPo3zNI-Cd_uCWqtZj4MYiaS2j_nBJ83VQ4tBIk7YuaXRkOMdsR8F3tRgxpF6EtSjHCyyoSfjSky339GFspP17_iidNPx6Pyyl3JmJp6Mr4yvtdbbkUPbMb5SgCszvpYX42ssO6UWmhY0L1BKg40JsAflvHcxgAtgyMpDlpa8rPlyBaQtlEClKI2Wuc2J7JXJQIlwyjfs7gD8B2F8lQmeT_zKK34ysUbtDBM_f7RHU81flM4uLeas7T5Pdzb034d0_q9S-a8eXgv_EwAA__8jaJ9y) he urged supporters to demonstrate peacefully. The BBC apologized in November and retracted the documentary, with its director-general and its head of news both resigning over the controversy. However, BBC officials said they would fight the lawsuit. Watch _The Telegraph_ explaining [how the footage was doctored](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0L1uwyAUxfGnwVssLh8mGRgqtRlSperW0cLmEhNhY5nroLx9lb5C13Ok3_D_ufZXF1Ncbl9uRksh9wvWkpAIt562fV7LjmUYxhWXJWKpeaNp7gUX-gDiAKb3LqZnrxpvA6JA2aAF0-lTd9JSNJMdnBRdGMejOR6DNoNXCtSoFJddAC1ME-1LAwEGgGsJ7aC81shRIgenAjDF0UfKW3vPcaEJQ3rmB27tmOcm2YloLUy-MXFm4lxrbZ95p33A1_9aHI0Tk-cHk-_ft8_79eLVx4U3m_VrjSnF0hmm-G12Mf2R9K8ODyt-AwAA__8zFnEO). A U.K. media lawyer said British courts would only enforce a maximum of $300,000 in damages, even if Trump were to win. The U.K. doesn’t recognize punitive damages, and the BBC has no profits or shareholders to draw from.
## Last US Pennies Sell For $16.7 Million
The final pennies minted in the U.S. sold for [$16.76 million at auction](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkMFuhCAURb8GdxgeiOiCRZPG3XTTNF0alOdIg2LgzUz8-8b-Qtf35t6c830bby7EsN8_3IaWljTu-CoRiTCPlB_bUR5Ypmk-cN8DllfKtG6jFFJzkBzM6F2I59hU3i6IElWFFkyr-7bXSlar9R5ct_gJvcGp0W52AsWinOug74VxVbDXGkgwAEIrqKfGa40CFQpwzQKsEegDpVz_pLDTiks80xNzPaetinYlOgpTb0wOTA5umi-C-p6umMnh65PJ4chIdPIL4uSPwmfcqfCCMXJoDd9CjCHtTA6FUj6ZGoJn6h1k1wDozlTZ-uN1lUprWCPumwvx757-5exp5W8AAAD___52gMk) Friday, with 232 three-coin sets each marked with the Greek omega symbol signifying the end of the coin's 232-year run. The most expensive set, numbered 232, sold for $800,000 and included the last circulating pennies from both the Philadelphia and Denver mints, as well as the final gold omega penny and canceled dies used to strike the series. President Trump ordered production halted in February after the U.S. Mint reported losing $85.3 million in fiscal 2024, making pennies that cost more than 2 cents each to produce. clean_body: President Trump filed a against the BBC Monday, claiming defamation after the British broadcaster aired doctored footage of Trump in a documentary days before the 2024 election. | | | | | | Wednesday, December 17, 2025 ---|--- Good Morning! On this day in 1903, achieved the first successful flight of a self-propelled, heavier-than-air aircraft, flying for 12 seconds and covering 120 feet. Before digital cameras and inflatable décor, snow days meant buckets, scarves, and whatever you could find for a face. photos in today’s edition capture that era. What kind of eyes did your snowmen have—coal, buttons, or cookies? Later today: watch for a partner message from on why Disney’s $1 billion AI deal matters, and what it could mean for you as an investor. If you’re feeling the year-end crunch, today’s sponsor, , helps you keep projects moving with fast access to top talent. | Fentanyl Named a Weapon of Mass Destruction President Trump signed an executive order designating illicit , directing the Pentagon and Justice Department to take additional steps to combat the drug that killed more than 48,000 Americans in 2024. The order describes fentanyl as “” than a narcotic, noting that two milligrams—equivalent to 10 to 15 grains of table salt—can be a lethal dose and could be weaponized for large-scale terror attacks. The designation allows the Pentagon to assist the Justice Department with defense assets to enforce criminal activity related to fentanyl, while the Justice Department pursues harsher penalties, including tougher sentences for trafficking. See . Trump Sues BBC for $10 Billion President Trump filed a against the BBC Monday, claiming defamation after the British broadcaster aired doctored footage of Trump in a documentary days before the 2024 election. The lawsuit alleges the BBC spliced together different portions of his Jan. 6, 2021, speech from nearly an hour apart to suggest Trump incited violence while he urged supporters to demonstrate peacefully. The BBC apologized in November and retracted the documentary, with its director-general and its head of news both resigning over the controversy. However, BBC officials said they would fight the lawsuit. Watch The Telegraph explaining . A U.K. media lawyer said British courts would only enforce a maximum of $300,000 in damages, even if Trump were to win. The U.K. doesn’t recognize punitive damages, and the BBC has no profits or shareholders to draw from. Last US Pennies Sell For $16.7 Million The final pennies minted in the U.S. sold for Friday, with 232 three-coin sets each marked with the Greek omega symbol signifying the end of the coin's 232-year run. The most expensive set, numbered 232, sold for $800,000 and included the last circulating pennies from both the Philadelphia and Denver mints, as well as the final gold omega penny and canceled dies used to strike the series. President Trump ordered production halted in February after the U.S. Mint reported losing $85.3 million in fiscal 2024, making pennies that cost more than 2 cents each to produce. . subject: Your Protest Is A Waste Of Time (Unless You’re Filming It)
body:
Protests without cameras, clips, and strategy are not activism. They are nostalgia therapy.

| |
---|---|---
| | | Forwarded this email? [Subscribe here](https://substack..FDDTVTMUsGPdUdHqvzVd2QAxYL98VNa69KpTO7vPZ84?) for more

[](https://substack..a9Qjgs0bp4KXDYh8kWTl4tdA1xiSfVQxJ5Dg1XuABOc?)
---
We need to find other **ANGRY DEMOCRATS.** Please consider sharing and subscribing to fuel the ANGER for change.
* * *
# [Your Protest Is A Waste Of Time (Unless You're Filming It)](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=180817037&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwODE3MDM3LCJpYXQiOjE3NjU5NzM2NjYsImV4cCI6MTc2ODU2NTY2NiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTMxNTkyNTgiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.UzzGGQma6BIWqFKYTnJCpW_Q6CIFhIbxDx-fqXR05oA)
### Protests without cameras, clips, and strategy are not activism. They are nostalgia therapy.
| | [The Angry Democrat](https://substack.com/@theangrydem)
---
| Dec 17
---
| [](https://substack.com/@theangrydem)
---

---
| | | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=180817037&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwODE3MDM3LCJyZWFjdGlvbiI6IuKdpCIsImlhdCI6MTc2NTk3MzY2NiwiZXhwIjoxNzY4NTY1NjY2LCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMzE1OTI1OCIsInN1YiI6InJlYWN0aW9uIn0.Vr7U283FKcfBc7REgWxAZIJaLNc2oYD8kSztRMsZY4w&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=1hvr60)
---
| | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=180817037&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwODE3MDM3LCJpYXQiOjE3NjU5NzM2NjYsImV4cCI6MTc2ODU2NTY2NiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTMxNTkyNTgiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.UzzGGQma6BIWqFKYTnJCpW_Q6CIFhIbxDx-fqXR05oA&r=1hvr60&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email)
---
| | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=180817037&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwODE3MDM3LCJpYXQiOjE3NjU5NzM2NjYsImV4cCI6MTc2ODU2NTY2NiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTMxNTkyNTgiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.UzzGGQma6BIWqFKYTnJCpW_Q6CIFhIbxDx-fqXR05oA)
---
| | [](https://substack..2N9vJVYTiWnnDuBS27oFfzCO6Yy0g0Vc7-U3lXqFpnM?&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email)
---
| | [READ IN APP](https://open.substack.com/pub/mattdiemer/p/your-protest-is-a-waste-of-time-unless?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app)
---

This is an unpopular opinion, but I did not create The Angry Democrat to shy away from opinions. I want to give my opinion, have you get angry about it, write back, and push the conversation forward. That is how ideas get sharper. Maybe you change my mind. Maybe I change yours. Maybe we both walk away smarter. That is the whole point.
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/ed4ae8a2-4a82-4723-a7b0-71115e2e85d4?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
My unpopular opinion is simple.
**Protests are a waste of time.**
With an asterisk.
Protests have not always been a waste of time. During the Vietnam War era, the civil rights movement, desegregation fights, and the women's rights movement, protests were the foundation of mass political change. In those decades, showing up in the streets was the clearest and loudest form of public speech available.
You could call your representative, but there was no guarantee they would listen. You could write a letter, but who knew if they would ever read it. Protests forced the political class to pay attention because media attention was limited and newspapers covered what they could see with their own eyes. A march forced a headline. A sit-in forced a conversation. A movement forced a vote.
That era is gone.
### **The Demographics of Political Clubs Tell the Story**
Go to any Democratic club meeting today in Northeast Ohio or across the state. You will notice the same thing I have noticed for years. The average age of active members is well into the 60s and 70s. In Wayne County Ohio, one of the most welcoming and hardworking clubs I know, the average age is easily in the 70s. That is not a criticism of older activists. It is a reality check about who is controlling the decision making.
You also see the same dynamic everywhere. Young people show up with ideas and energy. They speak with urgency. They want to be involved. What happens next is predictable. They are dismissed. Treated as though they are naive. Talked down to. Told to wait their turn. Eventually, they stop showing up. They form their own groups or disengage entirely.
This generational wall creates a political reality. The strategies being used today are strategies from 1975, not 2025.
### **What Does That Have to Do with Protests? Everything.**
Protests used to be effective because they filled a communication void. Now the void is gone. We live in a world where social media is the primary battleground. One viral clip reaches more people than a protest of ten thousand people ever could in the 1960s.
If a protest happens and nobody records it, edits it, distributes it, amplifies it, or ties it into a broader digital strategy, it dies on the sidewalk.
**A protest without a digital media plan is not activism. It is nostalgia.**
#### The Two Innovators Who Proved the Point
Barack Obama changed modern campaigning by mastering Facebook data and digital outreach long before political consultants understood its power. It was novel at the time. It mobilized young people and reshaped the electorate.
Then, years later, **Zohran Mamdani** reshaped campaigning again. He knocked on doors, made calls, and did the work on the ground. But what set him apart was a relentless, innovative, perfectly calibrated social media strategy. His TikToks, videos, and micro-messaging created a hybrid campaign model that is now the gold standard. He merged retail politics with viral digital communication.
His opponents ran the 1998 playbook. He ran the 2025 playbook. And he won.
### **Local Clubs and Activists Are Not Evolving, & They Will Be Left Behind**
Here is the truth. If your political club is not prioritizing digital media first, you are already behind. Having a Facebook page was innovative in 2009. Now it is the bare minimum.
Every club should have:
A TikTok.
An Instagram.
A YouTube channel.
A podcast.
Short clip editors.
On-site videographers during actions.
People dedicated to writing scripts, messaging, and tagging relevant officials.
Every protest should begin with the question:
**How will this be captured, edited, and distributed?**
If you are not asking that, you are not engaging in modern political persuasion.
### **The Hard Part: Trying New Things**
Trying new things is difficult. It is uncomfortable. If you are older, the world feels like it is moving too fast. Technology feels foreign. Social media feels embarrassing. But avoiding it is political suicide.
You do not punish your kids for trying something new. You encourage it. Activists need to do the same. You will make mistakes. Some posts will flop. Some videos will be awkward. But you learn and get better.
The alternative is irrelevance.
### **The Problem Is the Strategy.**
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/264e148c-695c-435a-8eeb-39377669ebec?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Protesting without a digital plan is the same as yelling into the void. It is not that protests themselves are useless. It is that protests _alone_ are useless in 2025.
If you want to change policy, if you want to pressure lawmakers, if you want your message to reach the people who need to hear it, your first, second, and third priorities must be digital expression.
This is not optional. This is the battlefield.
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/ee6425ed-1e25-4623-ae9a-59191ddbcf48?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Imagine fighting modern wars with line infantry tactics hahaha
Protests worked in the past because the world was different. If you are not combining your protests with a coordinated, aggressive, modern digital media strategy, you are not practicing effective activism.
You are reenacting memories of a political world that no longer exists.
If you want to win, if activists want to be heard, and if political clubs want to survive, they must evolve. They must empower young people. They must embrace technology. They must treat digital communication as the core of their activism, not an afterthought.
Because if they do not, someone else will. And when they do, they will outperform you, out-organize you, out-message you, and out-win you.
* * *

**Good Morning!** On this day in 1778, [Juan Martín de Veramendi](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LGSgyAQxvGnwU4HVg1aUFxjl2uvdBZYlBmCGdyL8e1vzCtc9a--X_H93Oc7xhTz8o0PMvwO25zp2BMxU5mfmFfMPlGIhRIe-2_kkHBZZpDQ1wpqpWePMZ1zV3ljpR9VRUbpWz9qGEaoVqP70Ttygxtkr8l5AGfRthBkB-HmQxXNhSlQWoFUrWy0bq1F9HJ0g1UKRCfJR95KwysxvXEP6dxeVBq3PapkVubnLtovAZOA6TiOhvcVt5xipmYri4Dps6o9nrU9rwiYKHO52vdtVYx_HjGluN-06OTywJg-OP_vkpeBvwAAAP__ZqR2Yw) was born in San Fernando de Béxar, now known as San Antonio. He became the Mexican governor of Coahuila and Texas in 1832. He was favorable to Anglo-American colonization of Texas, and his daughter Ursula María de Veramendi married James Bowie. ## Texas Sues Xcel for Deadly Fire
Attorney General Ken Paxton sued Minnesota-based Xcel Energy on Tuesday, seeking economic damages and civil penalties [for its role](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkL2OrDAMRp8GuqAk_ASKFLehm9tuiQxxwFJIEMlMhrdfMdp9gi1ty_58ztdjegA58ut_2FGntw2TxxwdpoTndIDfwBuHlk50kOOTknWwrpPksmVCMqEmA-SuqSmNnrkZRIlaqK4dlOwHWW4aurbvOULTyg5htpxLhcqCUU291LYtSd_HhBRKSC5qXilVzzOA4cPSz0LIouFoKIWzShsmfEO07govPKsl7KXTW0pHLOp_hRwLOeacq2WON8U9L-T4WSnkeLd-S2bJYWQ_TAxWIB8TeyZylC62hP0Af7E7hW0UUzhpYZmcuU2wBZ4RDZsvZkL2aNgRMp7Mkcc7oTy1OTI5R7FTRcPXHch9nk1_U_zS8jsAAP__2GKVQQ) in last year's Smokehouse Creek Fire. The fire burned more than a million acres in the Panhandle, killed three people and over 15,000 head of cattle, and caused economic losses of more than $1 billion. A Texas House committee report identified the company's decayed utility poles as the [source of the fire](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGypSAMhp8GOxyIHjkWFNvY3W23dKJEZERwgHs8vv2OZ2ef4DZpki_J__35Gr_QeRfsb9xJl_cSx0Bn9lQKpfHAsGIwnhaXyOOZv11ZPFo7goAHl8ClGg06f41tZfQkTC8r0lJ1j17Bs4dq1QJagEffTNKoTs5Gif6p0MBsepTYicrpe5kEqSQI2YhaqWaaEI3o5-ckJbBWkHElprqsVOiNefFXfFGq57hXXq-lHJk1vxgMDIbzPOvZu3mDNX7nEsM9xWC4YzEYPjyD4b7JYJBwl-5_g6PlGwV-4LvEwDPRlrnBHS1lvqS4cwqU7MXnuB8YLu5d2MjwEvk_3mOylAs_nTe3NAZDlbQ5Tue9y51irbA7Ov95vfxM-EvD3wAAAP__yK6WTw), and Xcel has "accepted responsibility from the beginning," according to a statement released after the lawsuit was filed. To date, the company has paid out $361 million for 212 claims. Paxton's lawsuit accuses Xcel of negligence, claiming the company had marked the decayed poles as needing replacement weeks before the fire. "_There must be accountability for the death and devastation the company caused_ ," [he said](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGypCAQRb8GM6agHQUDgk3I3qYbWq00Sj2UKWCe-vdbzi-8pKOuc-89_77GLwwx7Mtf3MjU06dxp6NEqpXy-MJ9xd1F8iFTxKO8Q_URl2UEAR2XwKUaHYZ4jc_GmUm4QTZkpOq7QYEeoFkNtN710PWDxEH7adZa6A6haz156ifdBHPDJEglQchWPJRqpwnRiWHWk5TAnoJcqCk_6kqVTiw-XumH8mNOWxPNWuursPYPA8vAHsfxwA1ziDHdDwxsqSlfDOy9jIG94xhYCffpGdgPk-PCX3jWtPPypsLPmSKnnfJycZ8yn_Fdwr7wNdy4MPOypW9a07sQnzPRN78lMbBaqUEp1QqhGNgmG_c6Qoyh9Io9xbJhiJ_i9Xe6fwz8DwAA__9kQ5Pe). Xcel is [denying](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGypCAURL9GMyy4OoMGBJuYzaYbWle4KDUIFjCj_v2W7xde1kHXqT797zW90HkXlr-4kSqnjVOgI3sqhdK0Y1gxGE_WJfJ45I8r1uOyTMDhwQQwISeDzl9TVxs1czOImpSQz8cgoR-gXpWGh7HaPrFHqfuWOrQa0Q6CD9rQjLVTN0yAkAK4aHkjZTvPiIYPup-FgKrjZFyJqSkrFToxW3_FL6VGx632ai1lz1X7p4KxgvE4jsbGs7s17kIF4x0rGN8U2I5niYHlD2V2avKMAqXlYjeOGULjL5a3-KY1fjIxnYje7LavkzL74bx3-Smrji8bOv8zoPzutq-C_wEAAP__oO-F8w) it was negligent in maintaining its infrastructure, promising to "vigorously defend ourselves against these allegations."
## Dogs Kill Man, Injure Mother and Child
A 62-year-old man was [mauled to death](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkL2OhDAMhJ8mdFklhiWkSHEN3V57JTLYQLQhrMD7w9ufuFe4zvJ8Gs3Mz627YUwxT9-4cJDPuHaZ33tiEd66B-YZMyUe48YJ3_szyphwmjowcNUWtHUdYUxHVxUUekPeFhysq6_eQeOhmENV-cEjGkK6NkRV7esG-8GXbJvGUVPEcJpZsM6CsaW5OFf2PSIZPzS9taAqwxRl3S4ys_AH9zEd64u3y7AuRQqzyGNX5ZeCVkE7nErM0x3lOAEF7dlIQXt-FLQLZr3gMzFpWTUxyqz7Q8u8MWtap12vWZ-snuOdNWbS_XnIhlFBW2yBHu-YUtxrpyozLRjTXxL5336vAL8BAAD__2lxhn4) by three dogs during his morning walk on Monday in Katy. After the attack, the dogs injured a mother and her 2-year-old son in a nearby neighborhood. Authorities [captured the dogs](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGOnDAURb_GdG9kP5gxFC6modsoUoqU6IGfwYmxR7ZZlr-PJr-w9ZXuOTq_P6YP8sHH9QftbOqXS1PkswSulfP0orhRtIGdzxzoLIevLtC6TijxDgpB6cmSD9fUNdbM0g6qYaP04z5o7AdsNrMwO9e33WzdQw36PiD3ZJW1PcrO3tvGm_eZQqUVStXKm9btPBNZOSz9rBSKTrL1NeVb3bjyFxUXrvTJ-bakvQlmq_VVRPsUOAocz_O87SW-N4EjRziKwHHJfmeB48qRMwWBo01rAUeVQrhgpyPAThF8hL9UL6hnglQ3zgV8_HNkthCZ8nzBRjn7Aks6Yr2gbJy9c1DoemMow_Opfr1097PJxr5OH4IvDy06ue7kw3_j-r3Onwb_BQAA___KZJUh)—two mixed pit bulls and a Cane Corso—after a four-hour search. They are quarantined and being observed for rabies. An upcoming hearing will determine if they are euthanized. Harris County deputies are [interviewing](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGSozAQRL9GZOPSjASCgOASsr30QmpAA6gskEvIy_rvr-xf2Liruvu9f1_jF4cYjvUv79KXnyWNh1xnlFIkjw8-Nj58lCVkiXydz1CWyOs6kqYakADd6DnE12gr30_ad1hJj66pO0dtR9XWL07M7K04a9AsxpJv2tYSoyZrUVMV-ncZEjokjUbfnDPTxOx1N7cTIimrxYeS8q1sUuSHzyW-0rfk25z2KvZbKY9TmT-KBkXDdV23-5ae71DRwLmEOYqi4c2laIhp5qho2PmAJT0PD17Yg08r7Px8qwAuhec7PFIukiEn9nDn8oLPtqKB2hqEl3YyOAFbnsBOLNA1xoKrHXqndYN1XeXeP64QYzgbp6xedw7xc7r8TvV3T_8DAAD__yibkNM) the dogs' owners to learn how they escaped and whether there's a history of prior attacks. No charges have yet been announced. "_I stood and hid my son behind me, but they kept backing me up_ ," [said Blessing Okwunwa](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLGuozAQRb_GdI6YMYmhcLEN3dt2SzR4BrDW4Mh2Hsnfr9hfeO090tXR-fM1fVGI4Vh_0y6uvpc0HXKWKLVKnp50bHRwlCVkiXSWV6hLpHWdsMW7BtRgJ6YQP1PXsJtbHqARB_ZxHyz2AzabM11nPImYHgzgYmzfMaLtBnjMcOdHE9x1BggWsAXT3qw180zE7eD7GQBV1wqHmvKtblLlTWWJn_Qt-ebT3kS31fosyvxSOCocafZgLqJwfKZSFY5n2unQfguRNdVK_q-wvqYlvQ7WLMSa06p3el0p9Cml6o1yDkX79DrqR5P3KfMFN1-SwhF67AfTtQrHJjt-niHGUB5Wde26U4j_3erPin47_BcAAP__PJmKog), the injured mother. "_Before I knew it, they jumped on me. I kept hitting and kicking them, but they were still biting me. They were not stopping_."
## Country Music's Joe Ely Dead at 78
Pioneering Texas country music artist Joe Ely [died Monday](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LuuozAQxvGngc6RZ7gYFy62SZdtt0QDHsCrCUa2c-Htj5JXOPUn_T7p_-823ihI2Ne_dGdX3kscd35l4VI4jQftG-1eeAmJhV75EcoitK4jauwUoAIzegpyjm3t3aS9hZodmL6zBgeL9eZswzh3PXYtGtu2zI1hCz1NaFueUNfBfTBAMIAaGn0xppkmIq_tPEwAWLWafSgxXcrGhd-UFznjk9Nljvda3FbKkavmT4XXCq8Hx0P4M1V4_R9ZsZzKM3lFKyszqCPFNXHO4cnqq6k5PvaSTiW88u4VwGA0WKiT88criITcm6rV652CfD_L70o9Hf4EAAD___OwfQc) at the age of 78 at his home in Taos, New Mexico. He has been hailed as a "progressive Texas country legend," a "Texas country-rock hero," and the "poet laureate of the open road." Born in Amarillo in 1947, Ely [grew up in Lubbock](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0L2uozAQxfGnMR3IM3wYChfb0GXbLdGAB_DKmMiehOTtr5JXuPVf-uno_LtNN_LBx-0vHWzltZ5T5CsHFuE03SnuFF3g1ScOdOWHlzXQtk2osS0BSzCTIx_eU1M4O2s3QMEWTNcOBvsBi90a1wwdY68JWui7WRNqgKVddQ9D3TeFtx8MEAyghlpXxtTzTOT0sPQzAKpGs_Nypkp2Fn5RXsP7fHKqlvMogt1F7lnVfxSOCsfruqo37ef5qQpHjsJJyMeDoygcj0f2i8KRkvglcFY4fs1ypsyu_OYy8MbRlf9PLnWHpjZa19UuRyiSdffLh-BzZ1Sjt4N8-O6Q3733tPgTAAD__75ng88) and formed _The Flatlanders_ in 1971 with Jimmie Dale Gilmore and Butch Hancock. After a brief stint with the Ringling Bros. circus, he released a self-titled solo album and began touring with punk rock group _The Clash_ , settling in Austin in the 1980s. During his career, Ely [collaborated with music icons](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LGuozAQheGnMZ2RPRAMhYtt6LLtlmhsD-DVBCPjhPD2V8kr3PpI35H-f_fpjpHjtvzFB9nyntO00XkwlUJ52nFbcQtMc8zEeB7PWGbGZZlAwU1qkNpMASNfU1sF61QYdEVWm-42GOgHqFYLPQUzUMAeUJEbVNP0jnwb3EBt1-sq2g-mQRsNSjeqNqZxDjGowfdOaxCtohBLynVZqdAbj5mv9KJc-_So2K6l7Ido_ggYBYznedZ-zWn7rALG5cmz9AmPImDEXKJnEjD-TySJLxkIg_yi0qfnVvIlQUPbaejqfd2rbMN-RuZ4dEa0anlg5O9v-V2tl4WfAAAA___VTH8t) like Bruce Springsteen, Linda Ronstadt, Tom Petty, and Paul McCartney. In 1999, he won his only Grammy with Los Super Seven, a group featuring Texans Freddy Fender, Flaco Jiménez, and Rick Trevino. Ely released his final studio album, _Love and Freedom_ , earlier this year. Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum CEO Kyle Young [said](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkDFuhDAQAF9jOhBewIbCRRq6S5sSLXgNGxmDYHMcv48uX0g_mpHm6zE8kCOn-RNXcvIK25DoOiOJ0DHsmBZMPlLggyJe5w9LiDjPA5TQ5BpybQePHO-hzrwbS9_pjJy2pukstB1ki2tMCDVOZpoIGpyCadtgNJIZTYXaY8buLdOgrYZSV2VhbTWOiL7spnbUGlRdkmfZjkIWEnrhGeK9Pekopm3NoltE9lNVHwp6Bf11XcV6n5gwyZZ4e0MKekpChyCnlZIo6PEQniIp6L83yineuWfyOWiom9rWxb7s2eH8fnGMfBqr6nJekeNfU_536ungNwAA__-7xH69) that "_Ely performed roots music like a true believer who knew music could transport souls_.”

For as long as the $2 trillion entertainment industry can remember, IP deals like this one have been a walled garden for a handful of billion-dollar studios. Disney buys Marvel for **$4 billion**. Universal buys DreamWorks for **$1 billion**. Now Disney invests **$1 billion** into OpenAI. It’s like a poker game where the minimum buy-in is the GDP of a small island nation. But the game is now changing. clean_body: Attorney General Ken Paxton sued Minnesota-based Xcel Energy on Tuesday, seeking economic damages and civil penalties in last year's Smokehouse Creek Fire. | | | | | | Wednesday, December 17, 2025 ---|--- Good Morning! On this day in 1778, was born in San Fernando de Béxar, now known as San Antonio. He became the Mexican governor of Coahuila and Texas in 1832. He was favorable to Anglo-American colonization of Texas, and his daughter Ursula María de Veramendi married James Bowie. If you play pickleball, Tesla would like to sell you its high-tech paddle for $350. Yes, this is real. Get the details . Later today: watch for a partner message from on why Disney’s $1 billion AI deal matters, and what it could mean for you as an investor. If you’re watching how AI is reshaping entertainment and wondering where everyday investors fit in, today’s sponsor, , shows how the game may be opening up beyond Hollywood’s inner circle. Busy day? Listen to our daily 15-minute podcast while commuting, cooking, or walking the dog—your Flyover fix, anywhere. | | Texas Sues Xcel for Deadly Fire Attorney General Ken Paxton sued Minnesota-based Xcel Energy on Tuesday, seeking economic damages and civil penalties in last year's Smokehouse Creek Fire. The fire burned more than a million acres in the Panhandle, killed three people and over 15,000 head of cattle, and caused economic losses of more than $1 billion. A Texas House committee report identified the company's decayed utility poles as the , and Xcel has "accepted responsibility from the beginning," according to a statement released after the lawsuit was filed. To date, the company has paid out $361 million for 212 claims. Paxton's lawsuit accuses Xcel of negligence, claiming the company had marked the decayed poles as needing replacement weeks before the fire. "There must be accountability for the death and devastation the company caused ," . Xcel is it was negligent in maintaining its infrastructure, promising to "vigorously defend ourselves against these allegations." Dogs Kill Man, Injure Mother and Child A 62-year-old man was by three dogs during his morning walk on Monday in Katy. After the attack, the dogs injured a mother and her 2-year-old son in a nearby neighborhood. Authorities —two mixed pit bulls and a Cane Corso—after a four-hour search. They are quarantined and being observed for rabies. An upcoming hearing will determine if they are euthanized. Harris County deputies are the dogs' owners to learn how they escaped and whether there's a history of prior attacks. No charges have yet been announced. "I stood and hid my son behind me, but they kept backing me up ," , the injured mother. "Before I knew it, they jumped on me. I kept hitting and kicking them, but they were still biting me. They were not stopping." Country Music's Joe Ely Dead at 78 Pioneering Texas country music artist Joe Ely at the age of 78 at his home in Taos, New Mexico. He has been hailed as a "progressive Texas country legend," a "Texas country-rock hero," and the "poet laureate of the open road." Born in Amarillo in 1947, Ely and formed The Flatlanders in 1971 with Jimmie Dale Gilmore and Butch Hancock. After a brief stint with the Ringling Bros. circus, he released a self-titled solo album and began touring with punk rock group The Clash , settling in Austin in the 1980s. During his career, Ely like Bruce Springsteen, Linda Ronstadt, Tom Petty, and Paul McCartney. In 1999, he won his only Grammy with Los Super Seven, a group featuring Texans Freddy Fender, Flaco Jiménez, and Rick Trevino. Ely released his final studio album, Love and Freedom , earlier this year. Country Music Hall of Fame and Museum CEO Kyle Young that "Ely performed roots music like a true believer who knew music could transport souls.” | Flying together with our sponsor What Disney’s $1 Billion AI Deal Means for Investors Disney just signed a $1 billion deal with OpenAI. The three-year licensing agreement will enable users to generate characters from Marvel, Star Wars, and Pixar using AI. But the real story today is the future of AI and IP, and . For as long as the $2 trillion entertainment industry can remember, IP deals like this one have been a walled garden for a handful of billion-dollar studios. Disney buys Marvel for $4 billion. Universal buys DreamWorks for $1 billion. Now Disney invests $1 billion into OpenAI. It’s like a poker game where the minimum buy-in is the GDP of a small island nation. But the game is now changing. While Hollywood giants scramble for their place in the AI future, already owns over 500 trademarks and copyrights, including iconic characters such as Cinderella, Snow White, Pinocchio, Peter Pan, Rapunzel, and many more. Their use of AI and other next-gen patented tech is on another level. They’re bringing their characters off the screen and into your living room with 3D holograms, headset-free VR, and AI-powered talking toys. And they’re . Note: This company is not affiliated with Disney. They spent a decade building this portfolio. Now they have a team that has executed $6 billion in merchandising deals in their careers, and they have already earned $15 million in royalties for Elf. Their proprietary interactive content, built on 12 cutting-edge tech patents, has just secured a $200 million TV distribution deal. As giants like Disney reveal their cards, everyday investors finally have a seat at the table. . | ➤ San Antonio: TxDOT has rejected the city's request to keep its rainbow-colored crosswalks following Gov. Abbott's directive to remove non-standard surface markings. The state denied a from the city of Corpus Christi. () ➤ Tarrant County: U.S. Rep. Marc Veasey (D-Fort Worth), who was drawn out of his district over the summer, has dropped his bid for county judge, saying he will serve the remainder of his term in an effort to "hold Donald Trump accountable." () ➤ Webster: A proposed 12,500-seat amphitheater is expected to create 400 jobs and produce a local economic impact of $3.7 billion over the next 20 years. The $150 million Sunset Amphitheater in Houston is being developed by VENU. () ➤ Baytown: Police officer Michael Hightower, 28, has been charged with manslaughter and aggravated assault after his cruiser collided with a minivan while responding to a call in October, killing two children and severely injuring two others. () ➤ San Antonio: Spurs legend David Robinson is accusing business partner Daniel Bassichis of misappropriating $34 million from Admiral Capital Group and two of its subsidiaries in a lawsuit filed Friday. () ➤ Amarillo: Thomas Hover, an assistant chief for the Irving Police Department, has been selected as the city's next police chief pending approval by the city council next month. Former Police Chief Martin Birkenfield resigned in February. () ➤ Austin: The Texas Comptroller's office has rolled out a new database that lists proposed bonds, the projects they're intended to fund, and the expected changes to property tax rates if they're approved. name: Marsha Blackburn
subject: Should I assume we won’t hear back from you?
body:
Peter, is everything OK?

| | | | Peter, why have you been giving us the cold shoulder?
I know I’ve reached out this week about this issue.My team tells me they’ve texted and emailed at least twice too!
Is everything ok? Should we take the hint and assume you aren’t interested in these messages?
That would be truly shocking to me since you’ve always been such a steadfast supporter of mine and President Trump’s mission to save America, so I’m giving you one more chance!
[My ONLY request right now is that you complete the Official Trump Voter Profile before the end of the week.](https://trk.emails.marshablackburn.) Really, that’s it! I just need your feedback, nothing more! | [ SUBMIT MY TRUMP VOTER PROFILE ](https://trk.emails.marshablackburn.)
---
You still have time, but not much, so I encourage you to complete your profile sooner rather than later! I’m also really starting to worry that we haven’t heard from you!

[Please, Peter, if you have a few minutes submit your Voter Profile before time expires!](https://trk.emails.marshablackburn.)

THANK YOU,
[](https://trk.emails.marshablackburn.)
Marsha Blackburn
[ SUBMIT MY TRUMP VOTER PROFILE ](https://trk.emails.marshablackburn.)
---

---

body:

name: The Editors at Broad + Liberty
subject: Ignoring biology won’t fix discrimination — but it will erase women
body:
News + editorial | Broad + Liberty Weekly Reads | | | |
---
| | | Good morning, and welcome to _Broad + Liberty_ 's Wednesday Weekly Reads!

As always, to stay up to date on late-breaking stories and analysis, [subscribe to our daily email list](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=bc272b0964&e=b7b3eddce2) and get the best of _Broad + Liberty_ delivered straight to your inbox.
---
|
---
|
### **1. ****[Ignoring biology won't fix discrimination — but it will erase women](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=ef52088d5c&e=b7b3eddce2)**

By Beth Ann Rosica Last week, seven Commonwealth Court judges heard arguments about the definition of “sex” according to Pennsylvania law. While I have been involved in this case as a petitioner since its inception, I was nevertheless surprised at the lunacy of the discussion. Let’s think about this — ten years ago, no one would have questioned what the definition of “sex” means. It’s a binary, biological fact. There are men with XY chromosomes and women with XX chromosomes. (This comprises the vast majority — over 99.8 percent — of humans on the planet while a minute number of people have chromosomal disorders.) Yet despite this common sense and widely accepted definition, the Pennsylvania Human Relations Commission (PHRC) changed the definition of the sex in 2023 to include gender, sexual orientation, and gender expression, amongst other things. The attorney for the PHRC argued that the Pennsylvania State Constitution does not define sex. Even one of the Commonwealth Court judges commented that the state does not have a definition for sex. **Why It Matters.** How did we get to a point in history where educated people do not understand this very basic fact? Attorneys are generally smart people, they must pass the bar exam, which is a rigorous test. Lawyers are taught to think critically and in a way different from most professions. And yes, part of their job is to find loopholes and ways to argue and support their case or client. Attorneys are not taught to suspend rational thought and common sense. Yet that is exactly what is happening in Pennsylvania and other blue states across the country. Even the most recently appointed federal Supreme Court Justice was incapable of answering the question, “what is a woman?” [_Continue Reading_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=90524bcf65&e=b7b3eddce2)
---
|
---
|
### **2. ****[Listening, learning, and leading with Dave McCormick at St. Helena-Incarnation School](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=ce5d38f88c&e=b7b3eddce2)**
By Guy Ciarrocchi Senator Dave McCormick was a leading co-sponsor of the new federal law that encourages donations to scholarship organizations, so that they can offer financial aid and scholarships for K-12 students, providing thousands and thousands of working class and middle income families with “school choice.” This mission is why he visited North Fifth Street in Philadelphia. Just as he had promised as a candidate when he visited Ligouri Academy in Kensington, Senator McCormick visited St. Helena-Incarnation School because of his commitment to school choice. To listen. To learn. So that he can lead in the Senate — and in the public arena. And, yes, to persuade would-be donors to give to scholarship organizations that support even more parents so that their children can attend schools like St. Helena’s and Ligouri. McCormick is quick to remind his audiences that he attended public schools and that his parents taught in public schools. This isn’t about public versus non-public schools. His cause is about empowering all parents so that they can find a school that works for their children. **Why It Matters.** McCormick genuinely believes in school choice — it’s why he co-sponsored the new law and continues to visit schools that will benefit. To learn from students and parents, teachers, administrators, and those who manage the scholarship organizations. During a roundtable discussion with students, they posed questions to McCormick and state legislators in attendance who champion Pennsylvania’s tax credit program. These included Martina White and Amen Brown of Philadelphia, and also David Rowe from Union County. Rowe’s home is a tiny county in central Pennsylvania whose population is smaller than the Olney section of Philadelphia — he was there to see how his good works make a difference. [_Continue Reading_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=5b5494bd78&e=b7b3eddce2)
---
|
---
|
# **3\. Lightning Round**
* [_From the Editors: Trump has a lot of good ideas, Congress should pass laws about them_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=1be73e4fe4&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Christine Flowers: A lefty podcaster’s grotesque attack on Erika Kirk_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=8dd419715e&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Harper + Lamb: New energy sources are needed now to combat soaring prices_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=4f3651c6eb&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Senate Pro Tem Kim Ward says Shapiro “might have a problem with strong women”_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=93ad4b4cc7&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Paul Davis: Feds warn of holiday identity theft, online shopping scams, and charity fraud_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=68ec8ae58d&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Thom Nickels: Judge not? Crime and punishment over the years._](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=4c684fcbeb&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Richard Kosich: The Dean of duplicity_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=5d0c9a8e36&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Nicole Prussman: How unfair policies are hurting Pennsylvania’s girls_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=26ed7479a4&e=b7b3eddce2)

---
|
---
|
# **4\. What we're reading. **
After enduring a month-long Democratic filibuster and government shutdown because of healthcare subsidies, the issue is back in the news again. At the _Free Press,_ Yuval Levin breaks down the problem and the question of why, despite the continual failure of Democratic programs, [_Republicans have been losing electorally on this issue for twenty years_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=6a81add32f&e=b7b3eddce2). Democrats thought they solved this problem with Obamacare and, when that began to fail, figured on their usual solution: throwing more money at it. Republicans, meanwhile, have countered with a plan to ... actually, they have no plan. And therein lies the problem: you can’t beat something with nothing. “So despite the fact that Republicans control the White House and both chambers of Congress,” Levin writes, “they’ve been lured into a Democratic trap because they have not developed any viable healthcare reforms for 20 years.” Democratic health insurance plans have failed time and again. But unless Republicans offer an alternative, the Dems will still have the best plan out there. And nothing will be fixed.
---
|
---
| [ ](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=108197a941&e=b7b3eddce2)

| | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
---
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

This real clip shocked millions — and it perfectly captures the world you’re investing in right now.| | | Don't want to receive our publications? | | | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18] | | | | | | A self-driving Tesla drifting down an empty road... Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18] | | | First Invеstmеnt Updаtеs brоught tо уоu bу Іnception Media, LLC. We would like to emphasize that we do not offer tailored financial advice. This email does not serve as financial guidance, and you bear full responsibility for your investment decisions. # [What Veterans and Athletes Know About Leadership](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=4399133&post_id=181885389&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxODg1Mzg5LCJpYXQiOjE3NjU5Nzc4MTcsImV4cCI6MTc2ODU2OTgxNywiaXNzIjoicHViLTQzOTkxMzMiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.Go3KL7E0lkofsOuaXwml3g2WzhVbPZe-Fbe_IRwmxr4)
### Warrior ethos and lessons from the Army-Navy conference
| | [Patrick J. Murphy](https://substack.com/@patrickmurphypa)
---
| Dec 17
---
| [](https://substack.com/@patrickmurphypa)
---

---
| | | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=4399133&post_id=181885389&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxODg1Mzg5LCJyZWFjdGlvbiI6IuKdpCIsImlhdCI6MTc2NTk3NzgxNywiZXhwIjoxNzY4NTY5ODE3LCJpc3MiOiJwdWItNDM5OTEzMyIsInN1YiI6InJlYWN0aW9uIn0.qOQBPd6ntIdkqmc25k7NLahqR0GdA64Ix457IQZ2Cc4&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=1hvr60)
---
| | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=4399133&post_id=181885389&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxODg1Mzg5LCJpYXQiOjE3NjU5Nzc4MTcsImV4cCI6MTc2ODU2OTgxNywiaXNzIjoicHViLTQzOTkxMzMiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.Go3KL7E0lkofsOuaXwml3g2WzhVbPZe-Fbe_IRwmxr4&r=1hvr60&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email)
---
| | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=4399133&post_id=181885389&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxODg1Mzg5LCJpYXQiOjE3NjU5Nzc4MTcsImV4cCI6MTc2ODU2OTgxNywiaXNzIjoicHViLTQzOTkxMzMiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.Go3KL7E0lkofsOuaXwml3g2WzhVbPZe-Fbe_IRwmxr4)

---
| | [READ IN APP](https://open.substack.com/pub/patrickmurphypa/p/what-veterans-and-athletes-know-about?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app)
---

Every December, we talk about home.
It's the place we're headed -- or wish we were. The people we can't wait to see. The ones we're missing. Home isn't just a phsyical address. It's a feeling that consumes our entire being.
For veterans and athletes, that feeling carries added weight. Home changes when the uniform comes off, the season ends, or the mission is complete. One day, you're part of a team defined by shared standards, purpose, and accountability. Next, you're expected to translate that identity into a world that doesn't always recognize -- or reward -- how you were trained to operate.
Veterans and athletes understand something fundamental about leadership that much of the modern world has forgotten: leadership is not about status or vision alone -- it is about operating under pressure, taking responsibility for outcomes, and belonging to something that demands your best even when no one is watching.
That tension sat at the heart of this year's Army-Navy Conference, which we hosted at Johns Hopkins Carey Business School on the Friday before America's Game. On the surface, the weekend was about football, cadets, pageantry, and tradition. But underneath it was something deeper: how athletes and veterans find belonging and purpose after their mission is over and what that journey tells us about the future of leadership.
For four years, the conference has become a gathering place for athletes, veterans, executives, and public servants who share a common warrior ethos. In many ways, it has become a home built on standards, service, and responsibility. That ethos matters more than ever in an emerging operator economy.

# How Governor Wes Moore Came Home to Baltimore
We were honored to welcome Maryland Governor Wes Moore as our keynote speaker. His story of perseverance demonstrates what happens when discipline, service, and accountability compound over time.
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/1f1f5884-2859-47a8-b62a-6170580d3152?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Moore spoke about growing up between Baltimore and the Bronx and how, as an adult, he realized Maryland was the place that felt like his true home.
Before he was governor and earned his many accolades, Moore faced real hardship. When he was three years old, his father died of acute epiglottitis -- a rare but treatable illness -- after being turned away from a hospital for care. That early loss shaped his understanding of how broken systems can devastate families, and why public service became a personal calling.
By the age of eleven, he was drifting off course and had felt the sting of handcuffs on his wrists when he was arrested for spraying walls with graffiti. His mother made the hardest decision a parent can make: sending her son to Valley Forge Military Academy -- not as punishment, but as intervention.
That decision changed the trajectory of his life.
At Valley Forge, Moore learned that discipline could become dignity, and that teams -- more than individuals -- can carry people through difficult times and change the outcome of a life. Those lessons followed him to Johns Hopkins, where he played football and studied international relations and economics, and then to Oxford as a Rhodes Scholar.
They also followed him into the Army.
Moore is a three-time Army volunteer. He raised his hand to serve as a teenager, volunteered for airborne school at Fort Benning, completed five parachute jumps, one carrying 30 pounds of gear, and another in the dark of night, and then volunteered to deploy with the elite 82nd Airborne 'All American' Division to Afghanistan. Over a 16-year career in the Army and Army Reserve, he served as a paratrooper and civil affairs officer, leading soldiers in combat zones and working on reconciliation efforts with local communities. He earned multiple commendations, including the National Defense Service Medal, the Afghanistan Campaign Medal, and the Bronze Star.
Today, at 47, Moore is one of the youngest governors in the nation, a bestselling author, and a leader focused on restoring trust in institutions and opportunity for families.
Moore's military mindset became visible to the nation in March 2024, when the Francis Scott Key Bridge collapsed in Baltimore, killing six construction workers in the dark of night and disrupting one of the country's most important ports. In a moment of shock, Moore moved immediately into emergency mode, coordinating response and acting with the calm urgency of someone trained to manage an unfolding crisis. It was the same logistical, mission-driven mindset forged years earlier.
That philosophy now shapes how he governs. Moore defines the most solemn duty of government simply: to keep families safe, physically and economically. It's why he's focused on building what he calls "lighthouse industries" in Maryland -- cybersecurity, technology, and advanced manufacturing -- industries that create dignity through work and offer families a stable future.
### **How Under Armour 's CEO Kevin Plank Built an Iconic Company in Maryland**
We were also joined by Kevin Plank, founder and CEO of Under Armour, whose story offers a different lesson about standards and resilience.
The youngest of five brothers, Plank grew up in Kensington, Maryland, in a household shaped by public service. His mother, Jayne Plank, became Kensington's first woman mayor and later served as Director of Intergovernmental Affairs at the State Department. Expectations were high, but Plank's path was anything but smooth.
He was thrown out of his first Catholic high school for academic and behavioral issues. He regrouped at another, spent a year at Fork Union Military Academy trying to get recruited as a college football player, and ultimately walked on to the University of Maryland football team.
It was there that the idea for Under Armour was born.
As a special teams captain, Plank hated changing sweat-soaked cotton T-shirts. There had to be something better. After studying the performance benefits of synthetic fabrics, he designed a moisture-wicking compression shirt -- the first Under Armour HeatGear® T-shirt.
Plank didn't start Under Armour with venture capital. He started it from his grandmother's basement in Washington, D.C., driving up and down the East Coast selling shirts out of the trunk of his car. By the end of 1996, Under Armour had made its first team sale and generated $17,000 in revenue.
Today, Under Armour generates more than $5 billion in annual revenue and outfits every Navy athletic team. Its motto, "Protect This House," isn't marketing fluff. It's a standard.
To Plank, brand is everything -- but only if it's real - he refuses to sell junk. Product matters. Story matters. Distribution matters. But values matter most, especially when the people wearing your performance gear are operating under pressure.
Plank addressed the Army-Navy Game being framed as a "battle of the brands," with Under Armour outfitting Navy and Nike outfitting Army. That framing misses the point.
This isn't Under Armour versus Nike. It's All Americans first -- young men and women who are scholars, athletes, and future leaders. More than 8,000 cadets in the stadium alone - every single one the definition of a student athlete. Proof that excellence, service, and ambition still coexist in this country, even when the broader narrative says otherwise.
# **How Elite Athletes Become Operators**
Former NFL running back, Justin Forsett, spoke to a truth many elite performers encounter once the game ends: excellence doesn't disappear when the uniform comes off -- but the systems that once demanded it do.
Forsett, who built a decade-long career through preparation, adaptability, and grit, understands what it means to operate without margin. His success was never about flash. It was about discipline, pattern recognition, and accountability -- traits forged in environments where performance was public and failure had consequences.
When Forsett transitioned out of football, the challenge wasn't work ethic or ambition. It was structural. The locker room vanished. The feedback loops disappeared. The daily pressure that once sharpened decision-making had to be rebuilt from scratch.
Instead of drifting, Forsett did what operators do: he rebuilt the system.
He applied the same mindset that sustained him in the league to entrepreneurship and investing -- creating structure where none existed, holding himself accountable without external enforcement, and surrounding himself with people who understood standards, not just ideas.
That distinction matters in what is becoming an operator economy.
As institutions fragment and credentialism weakens, the economy increasingly rewards people who can execute, adapt, and own outcomes. Titles matter less. Theory matters less. The premium is on people who can perform under pressure, navigate uncertainty, and take responsibility when conditions change.
Athletes and veterans are not adjacent to this shift -- they are ahead of it. They've spent years operating inside environments where preparation is constant, accountability is non-negotiable, and excuses don't survive contact with reality. What they often lack isn't capability, but translation and placement.
Former NFL long snapper and Army Special Forces officer Nate Boyer captured the connective tissue between sports, service, and society with a simple observation. Sports and veterans still unite America, he said. "You might spill a beer on someone in the first quarter -- but by the fourth, you're hugging."
That's home.
***

As the year closes and families gather, I'd like to remind you of something that's easy to forget: home may not always be a permanent, physical place. It can change over time. It's about finding a place where you don't have to perform to be accepted. Where you're valued not for what you produce, but for who you are. Belonging isn't something you earn, but something you're welcomed into unconditionally.
That's what we're building.
And it's worth protecting.

Warrior Class exists to do exactly that.

Warrior ethos and lessons from the Army-Navy conference | | | Dec 17 | | | | | | | | | | | | Every December, we talk about home. It's the place we're headed -- or wish we were. The people we can't wait to see. The ones we're missing. Home isn't just a phsyical address. It's a feeling that consumes our entire being. For veterans and athletes, that feeling carries added weight. Home changes when the uniform comes off, the season ends, or the mission is complete. One day, you're part of a team defined by shared standards, purpose, and accountability. Next, you're expected to translate that identity into a world that doesn't always recognize -- or reward -- how you were trained to operate. Veterans and athletes understand something fundamental about leadership that much of the modern world has forgotten: leadership is not about status or vision alone -- it is about operating under pressure, taking responsibility for outcomes, and belonging to something that demands your best even when no one is watching. That tension sat at the heart of this year's Army-Navy Conference, which we hosted at Johns Hopkins Carey Business School on the Friday before America's Game. On the surface, the weekend was about football, cadets, pageantry, and tradition. But underneath it was something deeper: how athletes and veterans find belonging and purpose after their mission is over and what that journey tells us about the future of leadership. For four years, the conference has become a gathering place for athletes, veterans, executives, and public servants who share a common warrior ethos. In many ways, it has become a home built on standards, service, and responsibility. That ethos matters more than ever in an emerging operator economy.
